title: Knocked-Out Teeth and Face Injuries: What to Do
summary: Learn how to handle a knocked-out tooth, a cut lip, a nosebleed, or a hard hit to the face — and when every minute matters.

sections.heading: Why this matters
body: Sports with balls, elbows, sticks, and falls can hurt your mouth, teeth, and face. This happens in soccer, basketball, baseball, hockey, and lots of other sports.

Most of these injuries look scary because the mouth and nose bleed a lot, even from small cuts. But there's one injury where speed really matters: a knocked-out permanent (adult) tooth. What you do in the first few minutes can help save that tooth.

sections.heading: What can happen
body: Face and mouth injuries come in different types. Here are ones you might see on a field or court:
list: A tooth completely knocked out of its socket — the big one, covered next
A chipped or cracked tooth that's still in place
A cut on the lip, cheek, or tongue
A hard hit to the jaw or cheek that might mean a broken bone

image.description: Simple front-view illustration of a young athlete's face and mouth area with four numbered callout points: one pointing to a tooth (knocked-out or chipped tooth), one to the lip (cut lip), one to the nose (nosebleed), and one to the jaw (hard hit/possible break). Clean, flat overview style, minimal labels.
image.alt: Illustration of a face with callouts pointing to a tooth, lip, nose, and jaw showing different injury types.

sections.heading: A knocked-out tooth: the most important skill
body: If a permanent (adult) tooth gets completely knocked out, every minute counts. The root — the part that was inside the gum — has tiny living parts that help the tooth reattach. Those parts start dying quickly once the tooth is out of the mouth.
list: Find the tooth. Pick it up only by the crown — the white top part you chew with. Never touch the root, the narrow part that was inside the gum.
If there's milk nearby, put the tooth in a cup of milk. Milk keeps the tooth's living parts safe.
Do not rinse it, scrub it, or wrap it in a dry tissue or paper towel.
Get an adult right away. The tooth and the athlete need to get to a dentist fast — every minute matters.

image.description: Simple diagram of a single tooth with the crown (white top part) and root (narrow part that was inside the gum) clearly labeled and color-coded. Next to it, a small illustration of a hand holding the tooth by the crown only, lowering it into a small cup of milk. Clean, plain illustration style for middle schoolers.
image.alt: Diagram of a tooth's crown and root, next to a hand placing the tooth by its crown into a cup of milk.

callout.title: Baby teeth are different
callout.text: If the tooth that came out is a baby tooth (not a permanent adult tooth), never try to put it back in. Just tell an adult — they'll know what to do next.

sections.heading: Cuts, chips, and jaw hits: tell an adult
body: For a chipped tooth, a cut lip or cheek, a bitten tongue, or a hard hit to the jaw, your job is simple: don't try to fix it yourself. Tell a trusted adult — a coach, athletic trainer, parent, or teacher — right away.

Don't try to push a chipped tooth piece back into place, don't try to stop bleeding with anything other than clean gauze or cloth, and don't try to decide how bad a jaw hit is. That's an adult's job, not yours.

callout.title: Signs it's serious — get help fast
callout.text: Tell an adult immediately if the face looks uneven or swells up fast, the mouth won't open or close normally, bleeding doesn't stop after a few minutes of pressure, or the athlete seems confused or was knocked out even for a second. These need a doctor right away.

sections.heading: Nosebleeds
body: Nosebleeds happen a lot in sports, usually from a bump to the nose. They look worse than they usually are.
Have the athlete sit up and lean forward a little — never tilt the head back. Tilting back lets blood run down the throat.
Pinch the soft part of the nose, just below the bony part, and hold steady pressure for about 10 minutes without letting go to check.
If the bleeding doesn't stop after a while, or there's a lot of blood, tell an adult so they can get medical help.

image.description: Simple illustration of an athlete sitting upright on a bench, leaning slightly forward, using two fingers to pinch the soft lower part of the nose. Clean, calm, plain illustration style for middle schoolers, minimal detail.
image.alt: Athlete sitting upright and leaning forward slightly while pinching the soft part of the nose for a nosebleed.

sections.heading: Prevention: mouthguards
body: A mouthguard is a simple way to protect your teeth and mouth during sports. It cushions your teeth and jaw from a hit.

Wear a mouthguard every time you play a sport where it's required or recommended, like football, hockey, lacrosse, wrestling, or basketball. It's one of the easiest ways to prevent a knocked-out tooth in the first place.

image.description: Simple illustration showing a mouthguard on its own next to a young athlete wearing it with a slight smile, showing how it fits over the teeth. Clean, flat, friendly illustration style, no text in the image.
image.alt: A mouthguard shown on its own and being worn by an athlete.

quiz.question: A permanent tooth gets knocked all the way out. What part should you hold it by?
choices: The root
The crown (the white part you chew with)
It doesn't matter as long as you're quick
Wrap it in a dry napkin and don't touch it at all
quiz.explanation: The root has tiny living parts that help the tooth reattach, and touching it can damage those parts. Always hold a knocked-out tooth by the crown.

quiz.question: You can't get to a dentist right away with a knocked-out permanent tooth. Where's the best place to put it?
choices: In tap water
Wrapped dry in a paper towel
In a cup of milk
In your pocket
quiz.explanation: Milk keeps the tooth's living root parts safe. Tap water and letting the tooth dry out both damage those parts.

quiz.question: A teammate's baby tooth gets knocked out at practice. What should you do?
choices: Do not put it back in — tell an adult
Push it back into the socket right away
Store it in milk and push it back in within an hour
Ignore it since baby teeth fall out anyway
quiz.explanation: Baby teeth are never put back in — doing so can hurt the adult tooth growing underneath. Just tell an adult so they can decide next steps.

quiz.question: Why does speed matter so much when a permanent tooth is knocked out?
choices: Because the bleeding never stops otherwise
It doesn't really matter, any time works fine
Only because a missing tooth looks bad
Because the root has living parts that start dying quickly once it's out of the mouth
quiz.explanation: The living parts on the tooth's root start dying soon after the tooth leaves the mouth, so getting it to a dentist fast gives it the best chance of being saved.

quiz.question: A teammate chips a tooth during a game. What's the right first step?
choices: Tell a trusted adult right away
Try to fix or glue the piece back on yourself
Ignore it if it doesn't seem to hurt
Rinse the mouth with soda to clean it off
quiz.explanation: A chipped tooth still needs an adult to decide what happens next — don't try to fix it yourself. Telling a trusted adult right away is always the correct first move.

quiz.question: How should someone with a nosebleed sit?
choices: Lying flat on their back
Sitting up and leaning forward slightly
Tilting the head all the way back
Standing and shaking the head side to side
quiz.explanation: Sitting up and leaning forward keeps blood from running down the throat. Tilting the head back is a common mistake that can cause choking or an upset stomach.

quiz.question: Which of these is a sign that a face injury needs a doctor right away?
choices: A small bruise that doesn't hurt much
Mild soreness in the cheek that fades quickly
The face looks uneven or swells up fast
A little redness that goes away in a minute
quiz.explanation: A face that suddenly looks uneven or swells up fast is a warning sign that needs a doctor right away. Small, mild symptoms that fade on their own are not the same kind of red flag.

quiz.question: What's the best way to help prevent a knocked-out tooth during a contact sport?
choices: Chew gum while playing
Keep your mouth closed the whole game
Avoid any sport that uses a ball
Wear a properly fitted mouthguard
quiz.explanation: A properly fitted mouthguard cushions the teeth and jaw from impact and is the single best way to prevent dental injuries during contact sports.

flashcards.back: A tooth completely knocked out of its socket. For a permanent tooth, this is a race against time.

flashcards.front: How to hold a knocked-out tooth
flashcards.back: By the crown (the white chewing part) only — never touch the root.

flashcards.front: Best place to store a knocked-out permanent tooth
flashcards.back: A cup of milk, if one's nearby. Never tap water, and never let it dry out.
The tooth's root has tiny living parts that start dying quickly once the tooth is out of the mouth.
Never put it back in. Just tell an adult — putting it back can hurt the adult tooth growing underneath.
Don't try to fix it yourself — tell a trusted adult right away.
Tell an adult right away — don't try to treat it yourself beyond simple pressure with clean cloth.
Tell an adult immediately — it's not your job to decide how serious it is.
Sit up and lean forward slightly. Never tilt the head back — that lets blood run down the throat.
Pinch the soft part of the nose (below the bony part) and hold steady pressure for about 10 minutes.

flashcards.front: Signs it's serious — get help fast
flashcards.back: Face looks uneven or swells up fast, mouth won't open or close right, bleeding won't stop, or the athlete seems confused or was knocked out.
A simple piece of gear that cushions the teeth and jaw during contact sports — wear it whenever it's required or recommended.
